name: AI/LLM Gateway Pattern

useCases.use: Any McCain application integrating with Azure OpenAI or other LLM APIs in production
useCases.dontUse: Developer experimentation in personal Azure OpenAI instances within approved innovation sandboxes

useCases.use: Multi-tenant AI features where different business units share LLM infrastructure with cost allocation per team
useCases.dontUse: Highly latency-sensitive real-time AI features where gateway hop adds unacceptable overhead — requires architecture review

useCases.use: AI features processing Confidential or Restricted data requiring prompt-level content inspection before sending to LLM
useCases.dontUse: Batch offline processing pipelines where synchronous gateway is not appropriate — use async queue pattern

components: Azure API Management — LLM request routing, authentication, rate-limiting, and token quota management
Azure OpenAI Service — primary LLM provider (GPT-4o, GPT-4-turbo) in Canada Central
Azure AI Content Safety — pre/post prompt content filtering for harmful content detection
Azure Monitor + Application Insights — token consumption tracking, latency monitoring, and cost attribution
Azure Key Vault — API key and endpoint management for all LLM providers
Azure Cache for Redis — semantic caching of LLM responses to reduce cost for repeated similar queries
Azure Policy — deny direct Azure OpenAI access from application subscriptions (gateway mandatory)

guardrails.rule: All LLM API calls must route through the centralised AI Gateway — direct Azure OpenAI access is prohibited in production
Token quotas must be configured per application subscription — no unlimited token access
Prompt injection and harmful content detection must be enabled for all AI features processing external user input
Prompt and completion content must not be persisted — only metadata (tokens, latency, model, application ID) may be logged
Gateway must configure at least two Azure OpenAI deployments for automatic failover on throttling or service degradation

revisionHistory.author: Priya Sharma
revisionHistory.summary: Initial AI/LLM Gateway pattern based on Azure API Management + Azure OpenAI integration

revisionHistory.author: Priya Sharma
revisionHistory.summary: Added semantic caching, multi-deployment load balancing, AI Content Safety integration, and privacy guardrails

strategicIntent: Provide a centralised, governed AI/LLM gateway that mediates all interactions between McCain applications and large language model APIs — enforcing prompt safety policies, cost controls, content filtering, and observability across all AI-powered features, while preventing direct unbounded LLM API access from application teams.

thirdPartyRisks.risk: Microsoft data processing terms apply to all prompts — data handling must be reviewed for Confidential data
thirdPartyRisks.mitigation: Use Azure OpenAI PTU (Provisioned Throughput Units) with data handling agreement; do not send Restricted data to LLMs

exceptionProcess: Submit AI Architecture Exception via ServiceNow with justification for direct LLM API access. Requires Integration Architecture Board and Chief Data & AI Officer approval. Maximum exception duration 30 days during approved PoC phase.
interactionSteps: Application team registers their AI use case in the APIM AI product catalogue with approved token budget, model, and content policy
Application authenticates to the AI Gateway (APIM) using its Managed Identity — no direct Azure OpenAI API keys in application code
APIM validates authentication, enforces per-subscription token quota, and checks if a semantically equivalent response is cached in Redis
If cache miss: APIM forwards request to Azure AI Content Safety for prompt inspection — harmful or policy-violating prompts are blocked
Clean prompts are routed to Azure OpenAI Service (or fallback provider) with load balancing across deployments
LLM response passes through AI Content Safety post-filtering before returning to the application
APIM logs token consumption, latency, model, and cost attribution to Application Insights for chargeback reporting
If primary Azure OpenAI deployment is throttled, APIM automatically retries against secondary deployment or alternative model
problemStatement: Without a central AI gateway, application teams integrate directly with LLM APIs (Azure OpenAI, OpenAI, Anthropic) independently — resulting in uncontrolled API cost, no prompt injection protection, inconsistent content filtering, inability to switch providers, and no visibility into AI usage patterns across the organisation.
securityConsiderations: No application may call Azure OpenAI APIs directly — all requests must route through the AI Gateway; enforced by Azure Policy and VNet isolation
Prompts and completions must not be logged in plain text in Application Insights — log metadata only (tokens, latency, model) to prevent PII leakage
Azure OpenAI deployments must be in a dedicated subscription with private endpoints — not in workload subscriptions
Prompt injection detection must be enabled via Azure AI Content Safety for all production AI features processing user input
Monthly AI cost attribution reports must be reviewed by the CCoE and shared with business unit owners for budget accountability
